findings.description: Header seal/logo graphics from the PDF are not preserved in the HTML rendering.
findings.evidence: On page 1 the PDF shows the North Carolina seal at top center and a DEQ logo at the bottom. In the HTML, these graphics are absent and replaced by text-only content.

findings.description: Some visual emphasis and indentation are reduced in the HTML, making the letterhead and numbered comments less distinct.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses centered letterhead with stronger spacing and more pronounced indentation for the numbered list. The HTML preserves the text and order but flattens some alignment/spacing.

findings.description: Indented parameter categories and sub-items are flattened somewhat in the HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 2 the PDF presents category labels such as 'total and dissolved metals', 'microbiology', 'nutrients', 'wet chemistry', and 'bacteria' with distinct visual grouping, followed by item 5 with lettered subpoints. The HTML retains the content but with less indentation and weaker distinction.

findings.description: List styling is less visually distinct in the HTML for the basin-by-basin comments.
findings.evidence: On page 3 the PDF shows a clearly separated paragraph followed by individual basin statements on separate lines. The HTML keeps the same sequence and text, but the visual separation is lighter and more paragraph-like.

findings.description: Signature image is not preserved in the same way in the HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 4 the PDF contains a handwritten signature above the typed name. In the HTML view, the signature appears only within an embedded page snapshot area and not as a consistently preserved standalone element in the reconstructed text flow.

findings.description: Closing block alignment and signature area formatting are simplified in the HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses centered closing text and signature placement. The HTML renders the closing as plain left-aligned text with reduced visual hierarchy, though the content remains present.

findings.description: The HTML does not use heading tags to represent the document structure; page headers and section-like labels are encoded as paragraphs/divs instead of an accurate heading hierarchy.
findings.evidence:
PDF presents clear structural labels such as "Certified Mail", page labels ("Page Two", "Page Three", "Page Four"), date, recipient block, RE line, and numbered sections. HTML contains no h1-h6 tags for these structures and instead uses

and

elements throughout.
